metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback storage state: specify that the dual-version reader must be deployed (and retained) independently of the v2 writer so that any rollback of the writer leaves already-written v2 records readable; document the safe rollback order and confirm that a v1-only reader encountering a v2 record either degrades gracefully or is never deployed without the dual-reader guard.
findings.finding: The plan asserts rollback 'restores the prior payload shape' (describing only the WRITER side) but does not assert on the storage STATE AFTER rollback — specifically, what happens when v1-only reader code is restored after v2 records have already been written to storage. If both the enriched writer and the dual-version reader are rolled back together, pre-existing v2 records in the sidecar store become unreadable by the reverted v1-only reader.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section — 'reverting a story restores the prior payload shape with no migration'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a deployment-order constraint to the Compatibility & rollback section: (1) ship the dual-version reader (v1+v2 acceptance) as its own story before the v2 writer story; (2) on writer rollback, the dual-reader remains deployed so v2 records already in storage stay readable; (3) document that rolling back the dual-reader requires first ensuring no v2 records exist in the retention window (or that the reader is only rolled back to a version that still accepts v2). This makes the post-rollback schema state explicit and safe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the specific numeric retention target (e.g. 'raised from 10 to 50 per ticket') in both the Success Criteria and a matching Acceptance Criterion with a deterministic oracle.
findings.finding: The retention-cap target is expressed as 'deep enough to audit' with no numeric value, leaving the executor to choose a number and making the corresponding success criterion non-measurable.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Review-sidecar retention is raised so a ticket's review history is deep enough to audit'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'raised so a ticket's review history is deep enough to audit' with a concrete value, e.g. 'raised from 10 to 50 sidecars per ticket', and add an AC line: '[ ] Sidecar retention cap is ≥ 50; oracle: a unit/integration test asserting the configured cap value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (with an oracle) verifying the raised sidecar retention cap — e.g. 'After the change, the cap is ≥ N; integration test seeds N+1 sidecars and asserts all are retained.'
findings.finding: The retention-cap raise is described as an IN-scope deliverable in both Success Criteria ('Review-sidecar retention is raised so a ticket's review history is deep enough to audit') and Scope ('retention-cap change'), but no acceptance criterion with a verifying oracle covers it — leaving it as uncovered work.
findings.location: Success Criteria (bullet 3), Scope (IN list), Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] The review-sidecar retention cap is raised to [N]. Oracle: a unit/integration test seeds [N] review sidecars for one ticket and asserts that reading the audit history returns all [N] records without truncation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback strategy for the retention-cap change (e.g., 'on rollback, prune only records older than the prior cap high-water mark' or 'keep the cap as a soft max to avoid destructive prune on downgrade') and document it in the Compatibility & rollback section.
findings.finding: The retention-cap raise is a behavior change whose rollback is destructive and unaddressed: if the cap is later reverted to 10, the pruning logic will delete the extra records accumulated under the higher cap — an irreversible data loss — yet the plan's rollback narrative covers only payload shape changes, not this scenario.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section; Success Criteria bullet 3 ('Review-sidecar retention is raised')
findings.suggested_fix: State that the pruning predicate on rollback will not delete records written under the raised cap (keep them as orphaned-but-retained), or that the cap is a forward-only floor (never decreased by revert). Document this explicitly in the rollback section alongside the payload-shape rollback narrative.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section) capturing the decision to enrich sidecars in-place, the v1/v2 dual-version reader contract, and the scope boundary (attestation manifest excluded).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel dual-version sidecar schema pattern, an in-place enrichment architectural decision, and a new audit read layer — none of which are captured in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc. Future maintainers have no durable record of WHY in-place enrichment was chosen over a new store, or the compatibility contract for v1/v2 coexistence.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a lightweight ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0042-lossless-audit-sidecar-enrichment.md`) covering: (1) in-place enrichment rationale, (2) schema versioning + dual-reader contract, (3) what is explicitly out of scope (signing, UI). Reference it from CLAUDE.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the new retention cap value (or range) and confirm it will be configurable (e.g. per-ticket-type or global setting), not hardcoded.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Review-sidecar retention is raised' but specifies neither the new cap value nor whether it will be configurable. Retention caps are operationally significant (storage cost, query performance) and typically need tuning per environment.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Review-sidecar retention is raised so a ticket's review history is deep enough to audit'
findings.suggested_fix: State the new cap explicitly (e.g. 'raised from 10 to 50') and expose it as a named constant or config parameter so operators can tune it without a code change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the deploy order for the completion-verification PASS-path change (agent emits before or after consumers are updated) and confirm that a feature flag or no-op fallback is available if the new sidecar causes consumer issues.
findings.finding: The completion-verification PASS path is changed from 'emit nothing' to 'emit a per-criterion audit sidecar' — a runtime behavior change with no feature flag or staged rollout path mentioned. If downstream consumers (e.g. the reducer, event processors) are not ready for the new event shape on PASS, the change affects all traffic at once.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'A completion-verification PASS persists a per-criterion audit record' / Scope — 'completion PASS-path capture + agent output-contract change'
findings.suggested_fix: Since the change is additive (new sidecar on PASS, existing FAIL path unchanged), confirm in the plan that existing consumers ignore unknown sidecar types (making the flag unnecessary), or add a one-line note that the read layer ships before the agent change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Broaden the plan title (or add a subtitle) to reflect both the write-side enrichment AND the read/retrieval API, e.g. 'Audit data enrichment — lossless capture and read layer for review passes'.
findings.finding: The plan title ('lossless capture of review passes') covers only the write/capture side, but the body also ships a full read layer (library + CLI + MCP audit API) that is in-scope per the Scope and Success Criteria sections. The title undersells the deliverable, which could cause scope confusion during review or handoff.
findings.location: Title vs. Scope section ('IN: … the audit read layer') and Success Criteria ('A documented read API (library + CLI + MCP)')
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Audit data enrichment — lossless capture and audit read layer for review passes'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion with a concrete oracle for the retention-cap change (e.g., 'A configuration/unit test asserts the sidecar retention cap is ≥ N, where N is sufficient to cover the full review history of a ticket through all five stories').
findings.finding: The retention-cap change is listed as a non-deferred Success Criterion ('Review-sidecar retention is raised…') but has no corresponding end-state-observable proof or oracle in the Acceptance Criteria — unlike the other three goals which each specify an integration test.
findings.location: Success Criteria ('Review-sidecar retention is raised…') vs. Acceptance Criteria section (no AC or oracle for retention)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] Review-sidecar retention cap is set to at least [N] records per ticket. Oracle: a configuration test or constant assertion verifies the cap value; an integration test seeding N+1 sidecars confirms all are retained.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider designating one enrichment story (e.g. halfbare-tsaristic-barnacle) as a sequencing gate that validates the shared v2 schema/versioning contract before the other two enrichment stories begin, to reduce coordinated rework risk.
findings.finding: No thin vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP sequences the riskiest shared assumption (the v2 schema design and sidecar versioning mechanism) before all three parallel enrichment stories (plan, code, completion) are built against it. If the versioning approach needs adjustment after story 2 starts, stories 3 and 4 may require coordinated rework.
findings.location: Decomposition — stories halfbare-tsaristic-barnacle, immoveable-tanned-snail, crumply-denim-pitbull (parallel enrichment stories)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sequencing note to the epic: 'halfbare-tsaristic-barnacle establishes the shared v2 envelope and dual-version reader pattern; immoveable-tanned-snail and crumply-denim-pitbull may begin once that pattern is confirmed.' This costs nothing and caps the blast radius of schema corrections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the reader schema guard in `latest_review_result()` (and its code-review analogue) must be updated to accept both v1 and v2 schema strings so the recall/re-grounding feature is not silently broken after the schema bump.
findings.finding: The existing schema guards in `latest_review_result()` and `latest_code_review_result()` hardcode exact schema string matches (`schema == 'plan_review_result_v1'` and `schema == 'code_review_result_v1'`). After the v2 bump, newly-emitted v2 sidecars will be silently skipped by these readers — breaking the recall/re-grounding feature — unless the reader logic is updated to accept both v1 and v2. The plan's Compatibility section says 'readers accept BOTH the old v1 and the new v2 records' but does not specify that the schema guard code itself must be updated to accept both versions.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Compatibility & rollback section (or the relevant child story), add: 'The reader schema guards (`latest_review_result`, `latest_code_review_result`) must accept both `*_v1` and `*_v2` schema strings — e.g. `payload.get("schema") in ("plan_review_result_v1", "plan_review_result_v2")`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that capturing `block_threshold` in the v2 sidecar requires stamping it onto the finding dict in `pass3_over_findings` (the review kernel), not only updating `build_payload`/`_slim()` in the sidecar module.
findings.finding: Adding the resolved numeric `block_threshold` to the v2 sidecar requires modifying the review kernel (`pass3_over_findings` / `pass3_decide` in `decide.py`) to stamp the threshold onto each finding dict at decision time — it is resolved there and currently discarded. The plan frames `block_threshold` capture purely as a sidecar payload enrichment ('it is resolved from the criteria registry at decision time and then discarded'), implying that only `build_payload`/`_slim()` needs to change. In reality, `build_payload` reads its input from the verdict dict's bucket lists, and `block_threshold` is never written into those finding dicts by the existing kernel.
findings.location: Success Criteria section ('the resolved numeric block_threshold'); src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py:384-391
findings.suggested_fix: In the relevant child story or the Approach section, add: 'The kernel change stamps `block_threshold` on each decided finding dict inside `pass3_over_findings` (e.g. `decided.append({**f, **d, "block_threshold": block_threshold, ...})`); `_slim()` then reads it out as a new field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the post-rollback state for the retention-cap change: explicitly state whether surplus records accumulated under the raised cap are pruned immediately on rollback, pruned lazily on next write, or left indefinitely — and confirm this is acceptable for the audit-trail guarantee.
findings.finding: The retention-cap raise (from 10 to a higher value) is described as a success criterion but the plan does not assert its post-rollback state: if the cap is raised and extra records accumulate, rolling back the cap configuration does not automatically prune the surplus records, leaving the store in an intermediate state (more records than the reverted policy intends) until natural expiry. The plan does not acknowledge this or state that it is acceptable.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Review-sidecar retention is raised so a ticket's review history is deep enough to audit'; Compatibility & rollback section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the updated schema-guard protocol for `latest_review_result` (and the analogous code-review reader `_latest_payload_with_ts`) — e.g. accept a set `{v1, v2}` — so backward-compatible reading is concretely defined, not just asserted.
findings.finding: The `latest_review_result` reader (plan_review/sidecar.py:157) uses a strict equality guard `payload.get('schema') == 'plan_review_result_v1'` to skip non-v1 payloads. The plan requires that 'readers accept BOTH the old v1 (rendered with the new fields absent) and the new v2 records,' but never specifies the updated guard protocol — leaving the novelty/recall consumer (`prior_concerns`, `surfaced_findings`) undefined with respect to v2 sidecars. As written, the guard will skip every v2 sidecar and fall back to an older v1, defeating the lossless-history goal for the recall/novelty path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Compatibility & rollback section, add: 'The `latest_review_result` reader's schema guard is updated to accept `schema in {"plan_review_result_v1", "plan_review_result_v2"}`; v1 payloads are returned as-is (missing v2 fields treated as absent). The same IN-set guard applies to `code_review/sidecar._latest_payload_with_ts`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate which previously-dropped Pass-1 fields (`evidence`, `scenarios`, and any others) must appear in the v2 `_slim`/`build_payload`, and whether the lean-sidecar design constraint is being relaxed for v2.
findings.finding: The plan requires the v2 sidecar to contain 'the complete pass-1 finding list (including previously-dropped fields)' — specifically `evidence` and `scenarios` — but the current `build_payload._slim` function DELIBERATELY excludes them with the comment 'deliberately exclude runtime-only carriers (e.g. `scenarios`, `evidence`, `_agentic`)'. The plan never specifies which `_slim` fields must be added, whether a new builder replaces `_slim`, or whether the evidence/scenarios are considered 'previously-dropped' in the v2 context. An implementer building the v2 payload has no spec for what the complete pass-1 record means in `_slim` terms.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the completion PASS-path audit record: name the new schema identifier, specify whether the agent output contract changes (and if so, what new field the verifier emits on PASS), and describe how per-criterion records are constructed when `findings` is empty.
findings.finding: The plan says 'A completion-verification PASS persists a per-criterion audit record (not only counts)' and lists 'completion PASS-path capture + agent output-contract change' as in-scope, but never specifies: (a) the new schema identifier (the FAIL sidecar uses `completion_verifier_fail_v1`; the PASS schema name is not given), (b) the mechanism by which per-criterion PASS records are produced — the `completion_verdict` schema defines `findings` as 'One finding per FAILING criterion (empty on PASS)' (completion_verdict.schema.json:15), so on a PASS the LLM currently emits no per-criterion data, and (c) what 'agent output-contract change' means concretely (does the verifier prompt now emit a new `passed_criteria` field? does the schema gain a new optional array?). Without these, the story author has no spec to implement against.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Scope / Acceptance Criteria / src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py / src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where `block_threshold` is attached to the finding dict — either 'stamped onto each finding by `pass3_decide`/`pass3_over_findings` before `_slim` reads it' or 'reconstructed in `build_payload` from the criteria registry at emit time' — and acknowledge the blast radius of the chosen approach.
findings.finding: The plan requires 'the resolved numeric `block_threshold`' to appear per-finding in the v2 sidecar but never specifies the placement point in the pipeline. Currently `block_threshold` is resolved in `pass3_over_findings` (decide.py:384) and returned as a local variable that is immediately consumed in the decision but is NOT stamped onto the finding dict. The plan gives no instruction on whether (a) `pass3_decide` / `pass3_over_findings` must be modified to stamp `block_threshold` onto each finding dict before `build_payload._slim` reads it, or (b) `build_payload` must reconstruct it by calling the criteria registry. These are different blast radii: (a) touches the shared kernel used by both plan-review and code-review; (b) couples the sidecar writer to the registry lookup.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py:384 / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:316
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer stamping in `pass3_decide`: add `f['block_threshold'] = block_threshold` after the decision branch (decide.py:~350), then include `'block_threshold': f.get('block_threshold')` in `_slim`. Note this touches the shared kernel used by both plan-review and code-review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit scope and AC to child 4e19 (plan-review) and child 7c84 (code-review) to capture and persist the pass-4 coaching output per finding in the v2 sidecar payload, matching the parent AC2 requirement.
findings.finding: Parent AC2 explicitly requires that each v2 sidecar payload contains 'the pass-4 coaching' per finding, but neither child 4e19 (plan-review v2) nor child 7c84 (code-review v2) mentions capturing pass-4 coaching output in their scope, ACs, or field lists. Both children enumerate: evidence, scenarios, block_threshold, blocking_enabled, pass-2 verification, pass-3 determination — but pass-4 coaching is absent from both. If the coaching bucket (a pass-3 severity classification) is conflated with pass-4 coaching (a distinct LLM pass generating per-finding coaching text), the v2 sidecars will not satisfy AC2's explicit four-pass completeness requirement.
findings.suggested_fix: In child 4e19, extend the _slim / payload-build step to include the pass-4 coaching text (or structured coaching record) per finding alongside evidence/scenarios/threshold; add an AC asserting the field is present in the emitted JSON. Mirror this in child 7c84's build_payload scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all existing consumers of completion_sidecar.py / COMPLETION_RESULT sidecar events in child e7e0 and confirm each handles PASS-path emission gracefully (or document the ones that need updates).
findings.finding: Child e7e0 introduces PASS-path completion sidecar emission, which is a behavioral change for all existing consumers of completion_sidecar.py — but it does not enumerate or analyze those consumers to confirm none will misinterpret a PASS-path sidecar as a failure indicator or encounter an unhandled schema variant. The consumer-impact mode requires a worklist pass over all consumers before analysis.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope item to child e7e0: identify callers/readers of completion_sidecar events (grep for COMPLETION_RESULT and latest_* reader call sites) and verify each is schema-tolerant or update it to handle both FAIL and PASS shaped records.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
